summary: Gruber analyzes the Wall Street Journal's report that Steve Jobs received a liver transplant in Tennessee, focusing not on whether the report is true but on who leaked the information and why. He notes the WSJ's extraordinary lack of sourcing — no anonymous attribution whatsoever — for what amounts to blockbuster medical news. He examines the suspiciously favorable timing (midnight Friday after the iPhone 3GS launch) and methodically works through three theories: a HIPAA-violating healthcare leak, a Jobs-authorized disclosure, or a board member acting without Jobs's permission. He concludes that a board member likely leaked the information, possibly against Jobs's wishes, believing public disclosure served Apple's interests.
key_points: The WSJ reported Jobs's liver transplant with zero sourcing — not even anonymous attribution — which is unprecedented for a front-page story at the publication
The timing of the leak (midnight Friday after iPhone 3GS launch, markets closed for the weekend) was perfectly favorable to Apple, ruling out a hostile source
The article's inclusion of speculation about the ethics of Jobs receiving a transplant liver suggests the information did not come with Jobs's blessing
Methodist University Hospital's present-tense denial ('isn't listed as a patient') rather than past-tense effectively confirms it as the transplant location
Details about Tim Cook potentially joining the board and weekly physician briefings to directors strongly suggest the source was on or near Apple's board
money_quotes: What is intriguing is the question of who leaked this information to the Journal and why.
Blockbuster news with no sourcing whatsoever. To call that curious is an understatement.
The basic tenets of journalism are simple. One reports facts and how one knows them.
It entirely hinges on the (admittedly significant) credibility of The Wall Street Journal itself.
If it's not true, it would amount to one of the biggest mistakes in their esteemed history.
Tennessee may be a lovely state, but, well, it doesn't sound like Steve Jobs country.
There is no other way to read this than as an implication that Steve Jobs may have gotten a liver that should have gone to someone else.

It sounds sensational to speculate that there is conflict in this regard between Jobs and at least some contingent of Apple's board of directors, but sensational or not, it makes more sense to me than any other scenario.
key_insight: The WSJ's unprecedented decision to publish completely unsourced medical news about Steve Jobs, combined with the leak's suspiciously Apple-favorable timing and board-level details, points to an Apple board member disclosing information possibly against Jobs's wishes.
